preFix.title: Making effective cleaning products from vinegar, bicarbonate, and castile soap
preFix.subtitle: What these ingredients actually do, what they cannot do, and how to use them correctly
preFix.excerpt: Bicarbonate of soda, white wine vinegar, and castile soap between them cover most domestic cleaning tasks without synthetic surfactants, phosphates, or plastic packaging. Understanding the chemistry, bicarbonate is alkaline and mild-abrasive; vinegar is acid and descaling; soap emulsifies grease, prevents the common mistake of mixing them together (which produces salt water, not cleaner). Each works best alone at the task it is suited to.
preFix.sourceNotes: Annie Berthold-Bond, Better Basics for the Home (2012). RHS gardening chemistry notes. Cleaning Product Information Exchange guidance on pH and surfactant action.

content.text: Three ingredients handle the majority of domestic cleaning when used correctly: white wine vinegar (5% acetic acid, pH around 2.5), bicarbonate of soda (sodium bicarbonate, pH 8.3, mild abrasive), and

action). The key rule: do not mix vinegar and bicarbonate in the same spray. They neutralise each other in an acid-base reaction, producing carbon dioxide (the bubbling) and salt water. Each works best alone.

White wine vinegar, descaling (removes limescale from kettles, shower screens, tap heads), cuts through mineral deposits, kills some bacteria and moulds. Best diluted 50:50 with water for general surfaces. Use neat for descaling a kettle (fill to cover the element, leave 30 minutes, boil, drain, rinse twice). Not suitable on marble, natural stone, or unfinished grout, the acid etches the surface.

Bicarbonate of soda, mild-abrasive scrub, odour absorber, light-duty grease cutter. Use dry as a scouring powder on baths, sinks, and oven walls; leave damp paste on a stain for 10 minutes before scrubbing. Pour with boiling water down drains to help deodorise, not to unblock (baking soda does not dissolve hair, grease blockages, or mineral scale in drain traps).

Castile soap, the best of the three for grease, oil, and protein-based stains (food residues, body oils). Dilute 1 tablespoon per 500 ml water for a general-purpose spray. Use 5 tablespoons per 500 ml as a washing-up liquid substitute. Works in both soft and hard water, though hard water reduces its effectiveness, in hard water areas (above 200 mg/l), double the concentration.

Disinfect to clinical standard. White wine vinegar kills some but not all pathogens. For toilet hygiene after illness or in households with vulnerable members, a dilute bleach solution (sodium hypochlorite) is the appropriate disinfectant.
Remove heavy rust or mineral scale from inside appliances without soaking time. Vinegar needs contact time (30 minutes minimum for a kettle, 2 hours for a washing machine drum).
Unblock drains. The standard bicarbonate + vinegar pour is dramatically less effective than a commercial unblocker or a drain snake.

White wine vinegar diluted 50:50 makes an all-purpose spray for a very small cost per use. A small pot of bicarbonate of soda lasts a year of moderate kitchen use. Castile soap concentrate makes many spray bottles from a single purchase. The saving versus branded all-purpose cleaners is roughly 60 to 85% per product replaced.
